words: SAM SCHRAGER: Can you tell us what booze fighting was like in the early years
when you were involved in the WCTU yourself?
CLARA PAYNE GROVE: Well, to go back much earlier than that, back to the very
early days after it was organized; the women met in prayer meetings and
then marched together to the saloons to talk with the saloonkeeper. And,
we didn't have fancy named for the saloons,Athey didn't at that time.
A saloon was a saloon! And women did not visit them. That was almost
unknown. But they would go to the saloons and talk with the saloonkeep
er, and some of the saloonkeepers did go out of business; not all of
them, but some of them did. But, at the time that I remember about Grand
mother, her family worship every day always included, "And put down the
wickedness of the saloons everywhere." Her family devotion never omitted
that. And, I presume there were many other people with the same thought.
And, I do not know, when I was a child, much about the working of it. But
I know that my mother was jail visitor. That's one of our projects.

speaker: SS
words: You told me about your jail visiting.

speaker: CG
words: I was jail visitor, also.

speaker: SS
words: As your mother was in her years.

speaker: CG
words: Mother was a jail visitor. That was a project. And we have a number of

speaker: CG
words: projects. For one thing, we have as allies, a group known as the Loyal

speaker: CG
words: Temperance Legion for children of grade school age. And at present, we

speaker: CG
words: are not having a Loyal Temperance Legion in Moscow because the woman who

speaker: CG
words: was leading it died a year or two ago and we have been unable since to

speaker: CG
words: find anyone who would take it on. And we also have a Youth Temperance

speaker: CG
words: Council for the high school age. And Moscow never had that group, but

speaker: CG
words: a great many places do have that group. And in one place in Oklahoma

speaker: CG
words: at one time, we had an extremely active National Director of the Youth

speaker: CG
words: Temperance Council work. And Oklahoma was dry and wanted to stay dry,

speaker: CG
words: but, of course, there was agreat deal of agitation to make it wet, and

speaker: CG
words: this woman went to Oklahoma and she would go to a Youth Temperance Coun

speaker: CG
words: cil group and tell them what to do, which they did, and Oklahoma went

speaker: CG
words: dry that year. And it was dry for sometime afterward. She had hand

speaker: CG
words: written stickers saying, "We cannot vote. Will you vote dry for us?"

speaker: CG
words: And there was a string in each one of them to hang them on the doorknob.

speaker: CG
words: And they hung them on every doorknob in the town after the town would

speaker: CG
words: be dark for the night And Oklahoma went dry that year, unexpectedly.

speaker: CG
words: That was one actual thing they did.

speaker: SS
words: That's interesting. When you went and talked to the saloonkeepers—

speaker: SS
words: I wonder what they could use, what kind of arguments they had, or how

speaker: SS
words: they would deal with the saloonkeepers, when they'd go in. Would they

speaker: SS
words: go in as a protest only, or were they going in to try to persuade them

speaker: SS
words: that they were wrong to sell liquor, or what was the thinking going into

speaker: SS
words: saloons?

speaker: CG
words: They would try to persuade them to not sell liquor. They would tell the

speaker: CG
words: men who were in there— try to get them to go home where they belonged,

speaker: CG
words: instead of sitting around in the saloon. They talked with everybody in

speaker: CG
words: the place trying to get them to run straight. And, we dabbled in pol

speaker: CG
words: itics quite a bit, because politics are partly what is doing everything

speaker: CG
words: that is done. So, we put push on our state to— well on our localcounty

speaker: CG
words: and state and national heads to work and vote and influence to

speaker: CG
words: rid the place of liquor. And, one congressman, — I think it was a letter

speaker: CG
words: to Maan (?) when she sent it, I frequently — in fact, just now I

speaker: CG
words: was reading a letter from Mc Clure— I think it was in a letter to

speaker: CG
words: me, I'm sure it was, that this congressman, and I don't remember which

speaker: CG
words: one it was, but it was of my state wherever I was at the time, said,

speaker: CG
words: "People may think it is no use to write to a congressman what their

speaker: CG
words: views on.anything may be, but it J^s, because before election, and before

speaker: CG
words: the vote on that comes up, he counts up how many were on the Yes

speaker: CG
words: and how many on the No side, and he is quite apt to vote with the maj

speaker: CG
words: ority. So, he said, "It is some use to write to your congressman, be

speaker: CG
words: cause you do force him to vote as you please." So, we do a great deal

speaker: CG
words: of that. I haven't kept very much touch with our legislators the last

speaker: CG
words: two or three years just because of inability to do it. But, formerly I

speaker: CG
words: was writing almost continuously to some legislator or head of a depart

speaker: CG
words: ment or a mayor or somebody; I was in touch with them all around a great

speaker: CG
words: deal.

speaker: SS
words: May I ask you, how much of a role do you think the WCTU played?

speaker: CG
words: What's that?

speaker: SS
words: How much of a role do you think the WCTU played in bringing about Pro

speaker: SS
words: hibition, in the first place?

speaker: CG
words: When we pray about it?

speaker: SS
words: No, no. How important was the WCTU in bringing about Prohobition in the

speaker: SS
words: first place? Did the organization have a lot to do with the enaction of

speaker: SS
words: that legislation?

speaker: CG
words: The WCTU was formed to work on the legislation of liquor; to work on it

speaker: CG
words: from every angle they could, from the individual in the gutter clear up

speaker: CG
words: to the President of the United States. At one time, the WCTU got up

speaker: CG
words: what was known as the Polyglot Petition. That was from all over the

speaker: CG
words: world signatures of individuals. I don't know how many there were, but

speaker: CG
words: I know that they bought a whole bolt of white muslin and they pasted

speaker: CG
words: all of these names, one after another, on that bolt of white muslin

speaker: CG
words: and rolled it again! (Chuckles) And two women in their surrey with their

speaker: CG
words: driver, drove to the Capitol to see the President. I wish I could rem

speaker: CG
words: ember which president, but I don't. But, anyway, at that time, anybody

speaker: CG
words: could see the President. You just simply went and rang the bell and said

speaker: CG
words: you wanted to see the President, and you got to see the President; that's

speaker: CG
words: all there was to it! Well, these women went to see the President and

speaker: CG
words: leaving the driver with the surrey, they went into see the President, and

speaker: CG
words: they told him that they had brought a petition to him. "Well, where was

speaker: CG
words: it?" And they said, "Would you please send a man out to get it?" Well,

speaker: CG
words: that seemed a strange request, but he sent a man out, and after a bit the

speaker: CG
words: man came back and he said to the President, "Would you please send an

speaker: CG
words: other man to help me? I can't carry it alone." And those two men came

speaker: CG
words: in carrying this roll of white muslin that had become immense. They

speaker: CG
words: said it was about the size of a big sheep! And they carried it in and

speaker: CG
words: presented it to the President with those hundreds of thousands of names

speaker: CG
words: on it! And, everybody all over the United States was writing to the

speaker: CG
words: President about the very matter, I do not recall what the legislation

speaker: CG
words: was that they were after, the exact piece of legislation. And, after a

speaker: CG
words: time, with women all over the United States writing constantly to the

speaker: CG
words: President And at that time, if you had a dozen letters you had a lot of

speaker: CG
words: letters. But he was getting them by the hundred and he didn't know how

speaker: CG
words: to manage it, how he could handle it, what he would do with it, and he

speaker: CG
words: finally called the President of the WCTU and he said to her, "Madam, if

speaker: CG
words: you will call off your women I will grant you anything you ask for!"

speaker: CG
words: (Laughter) So, you can see that we did have power! Well, Frances Willard

speaker: CG
words: lasted only so long.

speaker: SS
words: She was the founder?

speaker: CG
words: She was the founder, and the pusher, and the big IT of the whole thing

speaker: CG
words: as long as she lived.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know what she was like as a person? Frances Willard.

speaker: CG
words: She was small. And, as a child she was quite individual and quite active

speaker: CG
words: and always on the move and thinking of something. For instance, she had

speaker: CG
words: an older brother and he rode a horse, of course, the young men of that

speaker: CG
words: time, of course, had a horse and saddle as much as they do a car now,

speaker: CG
words: well, he rode and so she wanted to ride. And her father said, "No, lad

speaker: CG
words: ies do not ride." Well, they did, they had the sidesaddles and they rode

speaker: CG
words: them. But, that is how her father dealt with her. He said, "Ladies do

speaker: CG
words: not ride, you will not have a horse and saddle!" So, she rode the calves

speaker: CG
words: instead! Well, that was Frances Willard, you could stop her apparently

speaker: CG
words: but actually, you never did. And, also, at Christmastime each one had

speaker: CG
words: indicated what they wanted and the boy had asked for a book and the youn

speaker: CG
words: ger sister had asked for a doll, and she had asked for a slate and pencil.

speaker: CG
words: We all had slates, that's what we wrote on, of course, a child didn t

speaker: CG
words: write on paper, they wrote on a slate with a slate pencil! Well, she had

speaker: CG
words: wanted a slate, and on Christmas Day her brother— her father was very

speaker: CG
words: strict about the family just dressing up and sitting around for sabbaths

speaker: CG
words: and holidays, and so on— he was a very strict man about everything—

speaker: CG
words: there sat her brother reading his book; there sat her sister playing with

speaker: CG
words: her doll and her father said, "No, this is Sunday"-Christmas was Sunday

speaker: CG
words: also in that year—he said, "No, this is Sunday and you will not write

speaker: CG
words: on your slate on Sunday." Well, she endured it as long as she could, un

speaker: CG
words: til she had it all thought out. She took her slate and pencil over to

speaker: CG
words: her mother and she said, "Mama, could I draw on my slate if I didn't draw

speaker: CG
words: nothing but meetin' houses?" And her mother gave the father a stern look

speaker: CG
words: and she said, "Yes, Frances, and I will draw one for you for a pattern."

speaker: CG
words: Well, Dad couldn't say anything! So she drew meetin' houses the rest

speaker: CG
words: of the day. But that was Frances Willard; you thought you'd stopped

speaker: CG
words: her, but you didn't; you couldn't!

speaker: SS
words: Why? Were meeting houses that important? The meeting houses for prayer?

speaker: CG
words: A meetin' house was a church.

speaker: SS
words: That's what I thought.

speaker: CG
words: So she drew churches the rest of the day.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know where she came by her strong convictions about drink? Do

speaker: SS
words: you know what happened that she became interested in that?

speaker: CG
words: For one thing, her brother, Oliver, became a drunkard. And just before

speaker: CG
words: his death, he said to his wife, "Don't have any worry about me, I am

speaker: CG
words: all forgiven and I am all right." So that, he had been a drunkard; and

speaker: CG
words: I think that had something to do with it. But, also, she was always in

speaker: CG
words: terested in everything around her. And she never went to a school un

speaker: CG
words: til she was thirteen. There was no school, Vt^s the reason she didn't go.

speaker: CG
words: But the mother taught them at home, and when she was thirteen she first

speaker: CG
words: went to a school, because there was a school. And later, she and the

speaker: CG
words: younger sister were taken to some nearby town to go to a boarding school,

speaker: CG
words: And as they left the home, the hired man drove them in and he had a

speaker: CG
words: stove and a load of wood with them to put in their room in the dorm,

speaker: CG
words: he gave each one of them fifty cents, and Frances, very promptly spent

speaker: CG
words: her entire fifty cents for candy. And fifty cents would buy a lot of

speaker: CG
words: candy at that time; you could get quite a feft of candies at one cent apiece,

speaker: CG
words: and there were no ten cent ones. Well, that was one way. And

speaker: CG
words: some time or other, I think it was when she was in a higher school, but

speaker: CG
words: anyway, she was away from home in a school, and there were three of the

speaker: CG
words: girls in one room, including Frances. And the three of them decided

speaker: CG
words: that they would smoke!! So they got some cigars and started smoking!

speaker: CG
words: Well, someone tipped off the housemother about it, the matron, and told

speaker: CG
words: her about it, and so, she went and opened the door and stepped in and

speaker: CG
words: she said, "Oh, I'm so glad to see you smoking, the mosquitoes are just

speaker: CG
words: driving me wild, come into my room and smoke them out!" (Chuckles)

speaker: CG
words: They could do nothing else! They went into her room and she kept them

speaker: CG
words: smoking until they finished their cigars and they were so sick they could

speaker: CG
words: hardly get back to their own room! (Laughter) So they did't smoke any

speaker: CG
words: more.

speaker: CG
words: But Frances Willard was very individual. She did what she would re

speaker: CG
words: gardless of everything. She was small, and when she had had all these

speaker: CG
words: flings in her youth she settled down to be very quiet and sedate. And

speaker: CG
words: she had a very good voice and she said to be an unusually excellent speak

speaker: CG
words: er. That when she would go into a pulpit or a platform they would think,

speaker: CG
words: "Huh, that little thing gonna speak to us?" And in just two or three min

speaker: CG
words: utes of talking she would have them listening with all ears, and abso

speaker: CG
words: lutely quiet. She was a charming speaker, she could just take her audience

speaker: CG
words: with her no matter what she was talking about.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think she had to struggle to build up the WCTU and the movement?

speaker: SS
words: Or were there so many people that wanted such a movement that they all

speaker: SS
words: came right away?

speaker: CG
words: No, they didn't all come right away, but still, there were a great many

speaker: CG
words: wives who came because their husbands were drinking. So that it wasn't

speaker: CG
words: as difficult as might have been. And there hadn't been the propaganda

speaker: CG
words: from the liquor interests at that time that there is now, which makes it

speaker: CG
words: more difficult now because of the propaganda. But at that time, the

speaker: CG
words: liquor interests hadn't thought of such as thing as propaganda. And at

speaker: CG
words: that time, — now we speak of cider and hard cider— at that time if

speaker: CG
words: they spoke of cider, they meant hard cider, and then they said sweet

speaker: CG
words: cider. They had cider and sweet cider. Well now, we have cider and

speaker: CG
words: hard cider. But, at one time, the WCTU had the largest membership of

speaker: CG
words: any womens' group in the world; but it is not true now. The Rebeccas,

speaker: CG
words: I think it was, soon surpassed us.

speaker: SS
words: I want to ask you about how serious drinking was in those early days,

speaker: SS
words: around the turn of the century, before Prohibition came in? Was drink

speaker: SS
words: ing widespread; was it a very, very common problem?

speaker: CG
words: It was a very common problem. All men— you just pick out numbers of

speaker: CG
words: them, who were drinking. And at that time, there was no such thing as

speaker: CG
words: Relief, you sunk or swam according to your own ability, or if you would

speaker: CG
words: (phone ringing interrupted at this point) sink too far, you were put on the— No Relief

speaker: CG
words: or pension or anything,

speaker: CG
words: you earned your living as you went day by day, and took care of the gov

speaker: CG
words: ernment; just the reverse of what we have now! The government will take

speaker: CG
words: care of you.

speaker: SS
words: So this means that there were many people who were quite derelict be

speaker: SS
words: cause of the drinking?

speaker: CG
words: Well, as nearly as I know, they could drink til they were drunk today

speaker: CG
words: and tomorrow they were all right! I didn't hear anything about any— oh,

speaker: CG
words: what's the word I want?—alcoholic , I didn't hear anything about alcohholics.

speaker: CG
words: I don't know whether the people had more stamina or drank less

speaker: CG
words: or what it was, but I never heard anything about alcoholic until later

speaker: CG
words: years.

speaker: SS
words: Well, then what was it that was the real sin about drinking then? Did

speaker: SS
words: it tear the families apart? What did it do to be so evil?

speaker: CG
words: Well, generally, it did not separate people, because the marriage tie

speaker: CG
words: said they'd live together til death did them part, so they just did! I

speaker: CG
words: was in the eighth grade I think before I ever heard of a divorce. It

speaker: CG
words: was a rare thing. That was in Redfield,South Dakota and I don't re

speaker: CG
words: member hearing about any other divorce there, the years I was there.

speaker: CG
words: It was not common at all, because the marriage vow was held as sacred,

speaker: CG
words: and people just believe that it was, so if the husband got drunk, well,

speaker: CG
words: he was taken care of. And my mother used to tell about one case in which

speaker: CG
words: they sort of cured brother. He'd come home drunk every now and then,

speaker: CG
words: so this time they had homegrown, spun and woven and sewed sheets on the

speaker: CG
words: bed of linen; made it from the flax field. Well, new linen is quite rug

speaker: CG
words: ged, and when he came home drunk this time they got him in between two

speaker: CG
words: of those sheets and then they sewed the sheets tight up all around him

speaker: CG
words: so he couldn't move enough to struggle enough to get out! Well, when

speaker: CG
words: he waked and tried to get out, he couldn't get out, and the sisters had

speaker: CG
words: no sympathy with it, just let him stay tied, that was a good place!

speaker: CG
words: Wasn't going to get drunk there! So, he wanted water— "Oh, yes, sure."

speaker: CG
words: So they brought him a drink of whiskey (Chuckles) "No, he wanted water."

speaker: CG
words: So they took it away and brought it back again, and every time he wanted

speaker: CG
words: water, they brought him whiskey!! And finally, they had him to the place

speaker: CG
words: where he voluntarily said, "If you'll let me out of here, I'll never

speaker: CG
words: drink another drop." And he never did! That's the only case where I

speaker: CG
words: ever heard of a cure that way.

speaker: SS
words: What were the worst effects of drinking in those days?

speaker: CG
words: The worst of it was, that the sustenance and thought and work that should

speaker: CG
words: have gone to the home were not present. The man didn't have He

speaker: CG
words: didn't work at home as he should, and when he got any money the saloon

speaker: CG
words: keeper got it. And that was the mischief; the greatest. I knew one

speaker: CG
words: case over in North Dakota: A woman who had wanted to marry and have child

speaker: CG
words: ren, but she had no opportunity to marry, and it was said that her mar

speaker: CG
words: riage, when she did marry, was a heart-and-hand affair, which used to be

speaker: CG
words: so common, and this man, penniless, had married her for her money, of

speaker: CG
words: which she had considerable. I knew them when they had three or four

speaker: CG
words: children, and I was at the house one day and the little girl said, "Mama,-

speaker: CG
words: she was not home that day, she must have gone to a neighbors, and Daddy

speaker: CG
words: had gone to town. Well, after a while Daddy came home and he was reeling

speaker: CG
words: all over the place, he couldn't find the furniture and he had trouble

speaker: CG
words: finding where the door was. But, anyway, finally he said, oh, his stom

speaker: CG
words: ach was troubling him so, he had so much stomach trouble and it was both

speaker: CG
words: ering him awfully—excusing his condition to me, you know—. And pre

speaker: CG
words: sently, not so long after he came in, the mother came home and the lit

speaker: CG
words: tle girl ran to her— I admired that woman greatly— the little girl

speaker: CG
words: said, "Mama, Daddy's home and he's sick." The mother was teaching the

speaker: CG
words: children that Daddy was sick! So, the woman just said, "Where is he?"

speaker: CG
words: And the little girl said, "He went upstairs." Well, I excused myself

speaker: CG
words: and went home immediately. She was on her way upstairs as I started

speaker: CG
words: home. But she was really a superior woman. I always wondered why she

speaker: CG
words: hadn't had opportunities to marry. She was pleasant and agreeable and

speaker: CG
words: had education enough to be pleasant, at least. I don't know how much,

speaker: CG
words: but you knew that she had enough to do, and she had much money. But

speaker: CG
words: she'd had no opportunity to marry til she married this heart-and-hand

speaker: CG
words: man. Well, she had her home, a nice home, a big home, and she had her

speaker: CG
words: children and she had bought him, and she didn't repudiate him at all.

speaker: CG
words: She just took him as he was. And I thought she was jso sensible— not

speaker: CG
words: that I envied her any! But, I do think she had such good sense.

speaker: CG
words: And her teaching the children that Daddy is sick! I thought was just

speaker: CG
words: quite nice of her to do that.

speaker: SS
words: Is it a disease? Is it something that can be cured simply by willpower?

speaker: CG
words: It can be, if a person uses their willpower. There were a great many

speaker: CG
words: men who did quit drinking, because they would quit. For one thing, they

speaker: CG
words: didn't go back to the saloon; they avoided the saloon. And for another

speaker: CG
words: thing, the recognized in a way that they couldn't when they were full

speaker: CG
words: of liquor, what they owed to their home. When a man is full of liquor

speaker: CG
words: he doesn't owe anything except to another drink. But when they would

speaker: CG
words: quit drinking, they would gt a full realization of what they owed to

speaker: CG
words: their home and the misery that the home had endured while they drank.

speaker: CG
words: So there were a great many men who just cut right off;, they signed the

speaker: CG
words: Pledge and kept it. The signing of the Pledge was—, oh, it was talked

speaker: CG
words: as commonly as to have turkey $£ Thanksgiving is now, that so-and-so

speaker: CG
words: had signed the Pledge, or so-and-so was thinking of signing the Pledge,

speaker: CG
words: or something like that. And there was a great deal of pledge signing.

speaker: CG
words: And, at that time, a man's word was usually good, and if he had a writ

speaker: CG
words: ten signature with it, it was pretty sure to be good. It was really re

speaker: CG
words: garded— it wasn't just a bit of nonsense, like it is now.

speaker: SS
words: How did the signing of the Pledge work? Do you remember what exactly

speaker: SS
words: the words were?

speaker: CG
words: Well, I'll repeat the LTL— the Loyal Temperance Legion Pledge while I

speaker: CG
words: think farther.

speaker: CG
words: "While I live,

speaker: CG
words: Alcohol I'll never drink nor sell nor give,

speaker: CG
words: From all of tobacco I'll abstain

speaker: CG
words: And never take God's name in vain."

speaker: CG
words: That's the Loyal Legion Temperance Pledge now and I think has been a

speaker: CG
words: hundred years that the organization has been an organization. And, for

speaker: CG
words: the other Pledge, I cannot repeat it, but it takes in all liquor, in

speaker: CG
words: cluding beer, wine and cider. Well, at the present time, some women

speaker: CG
words: object to the word cider, but at the time the pledge was made, it was

speaker: CG
words: hard cider if you had said cider and hard cider contains alcohol, the

speaker: CG
words: same as any of the other drinks do. So that's perfectly logical to

speaker: CG
words: have the word cider included with the wine and beer.

speaker: SS
words: How would a man be persuaded to sign the Pledge? Would it be his wife

speaker: SS
words: who would persuade him, or would it be a delegation of the Womens Christian

speaker: SS
words: Temperance Union? How ould it come about that he would— had been

speaker: SS
words: exposed to it and come around to accept it?

speaker: CG
words: It might be from a pulpit or a platform that he saw the light and signed

speaker: CG
words: the Pledge. That wasn't uncommon at all, that someone on a pulpit or a

speaker: CG
words: platform would persuade various ones of the audience to sign Pledge.

speaker: CG
words: And, the women, even if they didn't belong, naturally were much inter

speaker: CG
words: ested in their husbands and brothers as to whether they came home drunk

speaker: CG
words: or sober. It made quite a difference. And the women of the WCTU would

speaker: CG
words: go to the saloons and individual men, so that there was influence from

speaker: CG
words: various quarters all around. And, it reached a great many men. People

speaker: CG
words: at that time were not like they are now. It was the common thing that

speaker: CG
words: people had backbones; I think that backbones are quite out of date at

speaker: CG
words: the present time. We depend on the (Bell ringing)

speaker: SS
words: Then than they do now?

speaker: CG
words: Well, they had to have, because there was nowhere to look, except among

speaker: CG
words: the neighbors, and they were mostly very neighborly, there was nowhere

speaker: CG
words: to look for aid. It was just as though you were on the midst of a lake

speaker: CG
words: with nothing but a log to float you. Well, what were you going to do?

speaker: CG
words: You were going to do everything you could about it. You were going to

speaker: CG
words: do your best. And you were going to get out, or sink. But this great

speaker: CG
words: FDR gave us the dole with the purpose of breaking the backbone of the

speaker: CG
words: American people! I'm giving my opinion, I'm not saying that everybody

speaker: CG
words: agrees with me, or that I'm absolutely right in all details, but it is

speaker: CG
words: my opinion that that was the very first move of the subversive elements

speaker: CG
words: that have planned, and are so close to taking the United States over.

speaker: CG
words: The dole that FDR gave us broke the backbone of the people, as it, in

speaker: CG
words: my opinion, was intended to do. Well, there was no dole,' there was no

speaker: CG
words: thing. There was the land, the sky, the water, everything was there

speaker: CG
words: for you to work with; go to work and make your way! And, since neces

speaker: CG
words: sity is_ the llother of invention, they found a way to survive, and to

speaker: CG
words: succeed, and some of them became very successful. But now, there is not

speaker: CG
words: the incentive to make themselves successful. The incentive is to see

speaker: CG
words: how much dole— now to me that covers, welfare and food stamps and a

speaker: CG
words: lot of things— there is the dole to look to. And there seems to be

speaker: CG
words: sort of a rivalry among people to see who can get the most out of it

speaker: CG
words: with the least return of anything for it. But there was no dole, there

speaker: CG
words: was nothing except his own self to look to.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that being poor was a matter of personal responsibility,

speaker: SS
words: rather than a matter of social conditions?

speaker: CG
words: It was personal. And, being poor, was just so common, and it wasn't

speaker: CG
words: looked upon as it is now. It was nothing to be poor. If you had some-

speaker: CG
words: body stop in unexpectedly and there was no place to sleep, the family

speaker: CG
words: just slept on the floor, gave up their bed, that was common. And, if

speaker: CG
words: there was nothing but corn bread and salt pork for supper, well, that

speaker: CG
words: was common. Nobody objected to it or thought anything of it. They liv

speaker: CG
words: ed simply and with so much less. There was one man right from here who

speaker: CG
words: said to his wife, "Our children don't know anything but to push a but

speaker: CG
words: ton. That's all they know." And he took them to Canada where he owned

speaker: CG
words: some land and there was a granary on the land, and there was one window

speaker: CG
words: in the granary and he took his wife and his nine children, including a

speaker: CG
words: babe in arms, to Canada and lodged them in that granary with one window!

speaker: CG
words: And there they lived until a year or two later, it seems that he gave

speaker: CG
words: them a house to live in. But they learned what it was to live without

speaker: CG
words: any buttons to push!

speaker: SS
words: I want to ask you about Ida Tarbel and her investigations of the trusts.

speaker: SS
words: Do you feel that there was much reason to be concerned about the power

speaker: SS
words: of corporations, as she seemed to be when she was working at Standard

speaker: SS
words: Oil?

speaker: CG
words: The power of appropriations?

speaker: SS
words: Corporations. Stand Oil. Remember Ida Tarbell and her investigations

speaker: SS
words: in Standard Oil?

speaker: CG
words: I remember about the Yes, I remember that.

speaker: SS
words: Was there evil from that side, from the side of wealth, too?

speaker: CG
words: I think perhaps about that time is when there began to be more of a mark

speaker: CG
words: dividing these people from those. I think that right there that scandal,

speaker: CG
words: oil scandal, had something to do with the division of the people and give

speaker: CG
words: us more people with less backbone than we had before. We talked about

speaker: CG
words: Teddy Roosevelt's "big stick" — did you ever hear of that? Well, it

speaker: CG
words: seems that the most he did with his big stick was to wave it in the air!

speaker: CG
words: Of course, he did fine Standard Oil, a terrific fine, I think it was a

speaker: CG
words: million dollars or something like that. And a million dollard then was

speaker: CG
words: beyond the comprehension. It wasn't like it is now. But it has been

speaker: CG
words: said that it was never paid. That it was understood that it wasn't to

speaker: CG
words: be paid; it was just to be assessed to the— well, I don't know the ex

speaker: CG
words: act truth of that— but if it was paid, I wonder how it became so per

speaker: CG
words: sistently known that it never was paid? And with people who should be

speaker: CG
words: an authority on the subject.

speaker: SS
words: Before we with Prohibition as a subject, there a couple of other

speaker: SS
words: things I wanted to ask you. One is, how much a part did relition play in

speaker: SS
words: getting people to take the Pledge? Was religion or Christianity very

speaker: SS
words: important in the movement?

speaker: CG
words: Christianity and religion were separate and apart above other things,

speaker: CG
words: worldly things. They were differently regarded than they are now. For

speaker: CG
words: instance, at the present time, there is a group that is rewriting even

speaker: CG
words: the scripture to eliminate the word HIM. If the scripture says him it

speaker: CG
words: should also say him/her. And there is a group that is rewriting every

speaker: CG
words: thing concerning the scripture and the scripture itself to eliminate

speaker: CG
words: 7him.' Well, at that time, no such thing had ever, that I know of, been

speaker: CG
words: even thought of, even conceived in anybody's mind. They were sacred

speaker: CG
words: things that were above, and if we touched them it was reverently, nevotherwise.

speaker: CG
words: They held a different place than they do now.

speaker: SS
words: Was conversion and accepting Christ very much a part of taking the oath?

speaker: CG
words: No, no, that was not a part of the Pledge and the oath and so on.

speaker: SS
words: But you say that prayer and prayer meetings were very much a part of

speaker: SS
words: the Womens Christian Temperance Union.

speaker: CG
words: Very much a part. Very much a part. And very much of Frances Willard's

speaker: CG
words: lifetf§he was the one who taught us that we have a foundation on the po

speaker: CG
words: wer of the Almighty. That was how she did what she did; attributed

speaker: CG
words: everything to the power of the Almighty as granted through her to do

speaker: CG
words: what she did. That was her teaching. And, we try to teach it now.

speaker: SS
words: When the women pray before going to the saloons, was the purpose of the

speaker: SS
words: prayer to give strength to their efforts?

speaker: CG
words: At that time, these women who held their prayer meeting and then went

speaker: CG
words: into a saloon, one of them would not have been able to force her feet to

speaker: CG
words: go into a saloon without preparation for it. They wouldn't have been

speaker: CG
words: seen in a saloon under any circumstances until now, with Divine direct

speaker: CG
words: ion, they were doing what they saw should be done, and they did it.

speaker: CG
words: And, as nearly as I know, they were always respectfully received. Not

speaker: CG
words: cordially, I don't mean that, but so far as I know, no disrespect was

speaker: CG
words: shown them. And, as for the present time, it's been perhaps twenty

speaker: CG
words: years or so ago, recent compared with these times speak

speaker: CG
words: ing of, there was a group of us coming home from State Convention and

speaker: CG
words: at one town somewhere, I don't remember where, the chartered bus that

speaker: CG
words: we had stopped for a considerable time and just across the street from

speaker: CG
words: the bus depot, there was a saloon. And, just as a sort of a joke one

speaker: CG
words: woman said to our State President who was with us, "Well, shall we go

speaker: CG
words: in and pray?" And, I said, "Well, perhaps we might." And the State

speaker: CG
words: President was utterly horrified at the idea that anyone would even say

speaker: CG
words: such a thing. There's the difference!! There's the difference in the

speaker: CG
words: terrain as it is now.

speaker: SS
words: When these people were first involved in the WCTU, were many of the

speaker: SS
words: women in the organization, women who had been involved in the fight for

speaker: SS
words: the right to vote?

speaker: CG
words: I do not believe that all of the women, by any means at all, believed

speaker: CG
words: that women should vote. I think there was a solid core of women who be

speaker: CG
words: lieved that, but not all of them. And, I think there were a few men

speaker: CG
words: who thought it would be alright for a woman to vote, but in general, not.

speaker: SS
words: But what I was wondering was, if the reform effort of the WCTU had been

speaker: SS
words: influenced by the struggle for the right to vote?

speaker: CG
words: I think not, perhaps. There was a little incident about a woman back

speaker: CG
words: in 1800; she was working through the county on votes for women, Mary

speaker: CG
words: Erliday (sp?) before it was common. She had one horse hitched to a bug

speaker: CG
words: gy, she was driving around the county trying to talk up votes for women

speaker: CG
words: and one night she was later than she^ thought she would be leaving a town

speaker: CG
words: and it was a very dark night, a black night, and that blackness just

speaker: CG
words: settled around her until she didn't know where she was exactly. She had

speaker: CG
words: planned to go to my grandmother's to spend the night, they were great

speaker: CG
words: chums, in their work and otherwise, and both of them mothers-in-law to

speaker: CG
words: the same couple. And, she had planned to spend the night there and she

speaker: CG
words: thought, well, she might just have passed it up and not have known it,

speaker: CG
words: and there was no use driving any farther. She unhitched her horse, tied

speaker: CG
words: the lines together, slipped the bridle out of the horses mouth, slipped

speaker: CG
words: the bit out, so that it was only a halter; tied one end of the lines to

speaker: CG
words: the horse and one to the wheel of the buggy; lay down under the buggy

speaker: CG
words: and went to sleep!! The next morning when it came daylight she waked

speaker: CG
words: and she was just half a mile from Grandmother's house! (Chuckles)

speaker: CG
words: Well, that's the way women worked on these things when they started.

speaker: CG
words: The fact that they were lost and it was dark and they had a horse to

speaker: CG
words: take care of didn't them any!

speaker: SS
words: There's one more thing. What happened after Prohibition came in as a

speaker: SS
words: law that it failed? Why didn't it work?

speaker: CG
words: Because the liquor interests worked harder and more successfully than

speaker: CG
words: the temperance people did. The temperance people had gotten to the place

speaker: CG
words: where we had Prohibition, they kind of sat down on the job. But the

speaker: CG
words: liquor interests didn't. They put on a furious campaign of vote for

speaker: CG
words: liquor. And I think their main cry, at least I heard it a lot, "We have

speaker: CG
words: to have it for the revenue." Well, let me tell you something, I don't

speaker: CG
words: know what the figure is now, but I know that at that time the dormant

speaker: CG
words: expenses that were caused by liquor was just one eighth of the— was

speaker: CG
words: eight times greater than the—

speaker: SS
words: Than the revenue they got from liquor.

speaker: CG
words: The revenue they got from liquor was only one eighth of what it would

speaker: CG
words: have been to— they put in welfare at that time, you see— and all this

speaker: CG
words: giving and giving and giving from the government— and the revenue from

speaker: CG
words: the liquor took up only one eighth of the expense. I'm not telling it

speaker: CG
words: like it— I don't mean that it took up only one eighth.

speaker: SS
words: I know what you're saying. You're saying that they spent eight times

speaker: SS
words: as much trying to fight liquor as they got from the revenue from the

speaker: SS
words: taxes.

speaker: CG
words: Yes. There were a great many people who believed that the revenue was

speaker: CG
words: paying for welfare. Well, it didn't at all! The revenue from the liqu

speaker: CG
words: or, and it never has paid for the expense of the accidents, that accord

speaker: CG
words: ing to court record and breaking what liquor laws we have and so on,

speaker: CG
words: the expense has been greater than the revenue. For instance, during

speaker: CG
words: Prohibition, people had liquor. I was at Troy at the time, I was in

speaker: CG
words: what was known as an apartment. It was two rooms and it had a wood cookstove

speaker: CG
words: and a table and four chairs and a sanitary couch; and that was an

speaker: CG
words: apartment. That was what I could get, where I lived for a while. Well,

speaker: CG
words: I looked out of my apartment window across Main Street down about a

speaker: CG
words: block or two and there they were passing a bottle and having a good time

speaker: CG
words: and one of the boys got drunk and was kind of wild. Well, it was well

speaker: CG
words: known in Troy who was furnishing the liquor and who was drinking it;

speaker: CG
words: but they drank it. At one time, not while I was up there, but another

speaker: CG
words: time, there was a woman who knew a man who was furnishing it, and about

speaker: CG
words: his trips and so on, and she knew that he crossed the railroad trestle,

speaker: CG
words: so one night she went and hid under the railroad trestle close up where

speaker: CG
words: it was attached to the land— she hid close in there and as this man

speaker: CG
words: came along she grabbed the leg of his trouser and hung onto him and he

speaker: CG
words: couldn't go any farther!! And she kept yelling for someone to send for

speaker: CG
words: the sheriff, that she had the man with the liquor!! But nobody sent for

speaker: CG
words: the sheriff, and she finally had to let go and let him go on.(Chuckles)

speaker: CG
words: Well, women do fight like that when they are fighting for something that

speaker: CG
words: they really believe in. They fight with tooth and toenail!!

speaker: CG
words: I was remembering some things way back in Redfield, South Dakota, and

speaker: CG
words: I remembered about the Joseph and Mary couple that came along there.

speaker: CG
words: They had no money at all; they had a team to a wagon, and they came in

speaker: CG
words: to Redfield and the man told the mayor that his wife could go no farther,

speaker: CG
words: that she must stop. Well, what was he going to do? So the town got to

speaker: CG
words: gether on it, and there was the James Building, a two story building,

speaker: CG
words: in which a man had had a store below and lived above, and he had sold

speaker: CG
words: out his store and gone back East. The building stood there empty for

speaker: CG
words: years that I knew of, but they put this family in there and there was a

speaker: CG
words: stove in the place, and whether the bed was there or whether someone

speaker: CG
words: furnished it, I do not know, but they furnished these people with enough

speaker: CG
words: that they could live and something to burn, and a couple of chairs and

speaker: CG
words: bedding, and fixed them so that they could live. And there the baby

speaker: CG
words: was born very soon after they stopped. And one day Mother sent me to

speaker: CG
words: town on an errand and it happened that this couple was in the James

speaker: CG
words: Building at the time. And the James Building, as I went downtown I saw

speaker: CG
words: was afire, the roof of it was afire. It was close beside the railroad

speaker: CG
words: track and undoubtedly a spark from the engine had ignited the shingles.

speaker: CG
words: Well, men with their homemade home ladders and pails of water put out

speaker: CG
words: that fire without much damage to the building. They soon had it out.

speaker: CG
words: Well, I had to stop and see and I remembered about this couple being

speaker: CG
words: there, and I thought, "Maybe I'll get to see that new baby!" So, big

speaker: CG
words: as you please, I was about ten years old,^I walked right up to the door

speaker: CG
words: and stepped inside and the fire was burning and the men working on it,

speaker: CG
words: and this woman was one elbow, braced up on her mattress on the bed, and

speaker: CG
words: at the four corners of that mattress stood four men holding to those

speaker: CG
words: four corners to carry her out in case the building burned. And as I

speaker: CG
words: stepped in she was up on one elbow leaning there and was saying something

speaker: CG
words: about her concern and one of the men told her, never mind, now,that they

speaker: CG
words: would carry her out instantly if they needed to, that there was a man

speaker: CG
words: that would run and tell them if they needed to carry her out, and I

speaker: CG
words: couldn't see any baby anywhere. I persume someone had taken the baby

speaker: CG
words: home, because it was nowhere visible.

speaker: CG
words: And by the way, these bumper stickers are not absolutely new. Ear

speaker: CG
words: lier even than that, by some years, there was a covered wagon that had

speaker: CG
words: a great, big, handmade sign on it that said, "Going home to wife's peo

speaker: CG
words: ple!" (Laughter) Well, I knew later than that, many years later than

speaker: CG
words: that, I knew a couple right in our town of Moore that had to go home,

speaker: CG
words: East to wife's people. And they had a dog that it broke their hearts to

speaker: CG
words: leave— wife's people were sending them money to go home on, and they

speaker: CG
words: said they would not pay for taking the dog home,;wouldn't have it when

speaker: CG
words: they got it there. So my girl took that dog and when we didn't have

speaker: CG
words: any people to go to but^had to go, she had to give up her dog. It hurt

speaker: CG
words: her awfully. We were leaving and there was nothing else to do. But

speaker: CG
words: this was a man and wife and I think one child, a little girl. And I

speaker: CG
words: have in that china cupboard to this day some pieces that I bought, not

speaker: CG
words: because I wanted them especially or anything, but to give them that much

speaker: CG
words: more money to go on. They sold everything but what they had on. All

speaker: CG
words: their housekeeping things to help out on their ticket East.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have to go East because they had no money to stay in the West?

speaker: CG
words: They had no money and he tried to make a living, but nobody had a liv

speaker: CG
words: ing at that time. Hard times had struck. There just wasn't money.

speaker: SS
words: Is that why you had to leave Moore?

speaker: CG
words: Well, I had no money and a brother in North Washington had offered his

speaker: CG
words: home to me. He was away, so we went to his home in North Washington.

speaker: CG
words: I did have an enormous sum for myself and my daughter, who was in grade

speaker: CG
words: school, upper grades,— I had the enormous sum of five dollars a month

speaker: CG
words: for food and clothing and school and everything. But we got by some

speaker: CG
words: way.

speaker: SS
words: May I ask— what hap—

speaker: CG
words: I told you about the boys in Troy that didn't understand—? Didn't I?

speaker: CG
words: When the man was stuck in the mud?

speaker: SS
words: No.

speaker: CG
words: Well, I had better tell you that. This was in Troy in 1925 or '26, and

speaker: CG
words: the streets were not improved much and there was an intersection that

speaker: CG
words: was just a loblolly of mud, and a man alone in a car got stuck in there

speaker: CG
words: and he couldn't sit behind the wheel and push both at once, and there

speaker: CG
words: was no other way to get out. And there were two young boys, Swedes from

speaker: CG
words: Sweden, who were visiting friends in Troy. They had just come there,

speaker: CG
words: and they had asked these friends here what they would do, that they knew

speaker: CG
words: no English at all, and what could they do? So this man said, "Now you

speaker: CG
words: just listen and say what I say, you learn it." And he said to say, "I

speaker: CG
words: do not understand you." Well, they practiced that til they could say,

speaker: CG
words: "I do not understand you." And they were beautifully dressed. Troy

speaker: CG
words: had never seen such dressing as those boys wore. And this morning, I

speaker: CG
words: was at my printshop office, so I saw the whole thing out on Main Street.

speaker: CG
words: These boys were coming down the hill downtown and picking their way

speaker: CG
words: carefully not to get their shoes muddied, and when they got to this man

speaker: CG
words: he thought, of course, they'd stop and help him, but they walked right

speaker: CG
words: along and he asked them to stop and help. And they said, "I do not un

speaker: CG
words: derstand you." And he motioned then that he wanted them to push the

speaker: CG
words: car. "I do not understand you." (Chuckles) And that's what they could

speaker: CG
words: say and they went on saying, "I do not understand you!" And there he

speaker: CG
words: was, still stuck in the mud! And I had to go back to my type.

speaker: CG
words: But there were some other things, too. When talking about

speaker: CG
words: Troy the other evening, I thought of these things that had happened while

speaker: CG
words: I was there. Oh, let's see— I have a lot of different things on here.

speaker: CG
words: Oh, I had just gone to Troy and I was working half days in the print

speaker: CG
words: shop before I bought it. I was going down the street early to the print

speaker: CG
words: shop , and the men of the brickplant were just going to work: Well, -

speaker: CG
words: here was a woman, a stranger in the town- everyone of those men going

speaker: CG
words: to work at the brickplant stepped to the edge of the sidewalk, stopped

speaker: CG
words: and lifted his hat until I passed. Can you think of a more beautiful

speaker: CG
words: reception to a stranger in a town? I was just amazed. It probably was

speaker: CG
words: an old country custom. I never knew of such a thing here excepting that.

speaker: CG
words: I had something else, too.

speaker: SS
words: I've got some things I wanted to ask you about. One, I wanted to ask

speaker: SS
words: you about is— Can you thinking back remember what some of your very

speaker: SS
words: earliest memories were, when you were just a very small child? When

speaker: SS
words: you were say, five or younger even? What some of the first things you

speaker: SS
words: remember are?

speaker: CG
words: The first thing I remember, I must have been perhaps three years old,—

speaker: CG
words: I didn't tell you about the time I hid in the well, did I? Well, I

speaker: CG
words: think I was about three years old, and you can believe it or not, I used

speaker: CG
words: to be so timid and bashful that if I had to meet a stranger I would be

speaker: CG
words: physically ill from it. You wouldn't think it now, would you? (Chuckles)

speaker: CG
words: But, anyway, that's the way I was. Two women had come in to visit with

speaker: CG
words: Mother one afternoon, and they kept talking about oh, "What beautiful

speaker: CG
words: curls! And she has blue eyes, too." It's a great detriment to a child

speaker: CG
words: to have gold curls and blue eyes! Snub nosed, freckled face little thing

speaker: CG
words: with carroty hair is the one that's lucky because people let her live

speaker: CG
words: her own life! But, anyway, they kept that up and I was so frightened,

speaker: CG
words: and I clung to Mother until finally they got busy talking among them

speaker: CG
words: selves and I hid behind Mother's chair, and when they got very busy talk

speaker: CG
words: ing I slipped out for a place to hide, so I wouldn't see them at all.

speaker: CG
words: And we had a well not very far from the backdoor, at which they were

speaker: CG
words: sitting, and I went out to the well and there was a platform, a square

speaker: CG
words: platform, laid over the well, and then^£:rnerwise on this had been set

speaker: CG
words: a square curb that stood up, so that inside of the curb there were four

speaker: CG
words: triangles of board. Well, I thought that would be a good place to hide.

speaker: CG
words: So I can remember how I struggled to lift myself up over the curb, I

speaker: CG
words: got hold of it with both hands and I struggled til I got a foot over

speaker: CG
words: and.I got over, and I crouched down on that little three cornered piece

speaker: CG
words: of board, right there, right above the water, and there I lay. And

speaker: CG
words: after a while I heard Mother say, "I wonder where Clara is? I guess I

speaker: CG
words: better go and see." Well, the three of them got up and wandered about

speaker: CG
words: the yard calling my name, and Mother would call me and I'd start to rise

speaker: CG
words: and these two women would call my name, and I'd crouch down again. And

speaker: CG
words: finally, Mother said, "Well, I guess I'll have to look in the well."

speaker: CG
words: And I thought, "What's the matter with Mother that she talks so funny?"

speaker: CG
words: Her voice wasn't natural at all. But, anyway, she came to look in the

speaker: CG
words: well, and I couldn't understand why she would grab my arm so tight that

speaker: CG
words: it hurt. Mother hurting me, I couldn't see why. But anyway, she picked

speaker: CG
words: me out of that well in a hurry! (Laughter) I was safely hidden there!

speaker: SS
words: Did she punish you for something like that?

speaker: CG
words: No. No, I was the family pet, unfortunately. It was awfully hard on me,

speaker: CG
words: but I was.

speaker: SS
words: Is it unfortunate to be the family pet? As a child?

speaker: CG
words: Well, there were seven of us all told, and I was the pet, because the

speaker: CG
words: sixth one was eleven years later than myself. But, anyway, if people

speaker: CG
words: just understood how unfortunate it is for the child to make a pet of

speaker: CG
words: it.

speaker: SS
words: Spoiling it, you mean?

speaker: CG
words: Well, I think a little good spoiling is good for a child, but I think

speaker: CG
words: it should be limited to very little. But, it wasn't with me, I pretty

speaker: CG
words: much had my own way.

speaker: SS
words: Well, doesn't that help to make an independent, strong-willed person

speaker: SS
words: if she gets her own way?

speaker: CG
words: It floors you. It leaves you without any personality. It leaves you

speaker: CG
words: without a mind. I had an older sister, and oldest sister, who felt that

speaker: CG
words: she was the one me; she knew how. She was eight years older

speaker: CG
words: than I was. I couldn't do one thing that she wasn't on my neck about it.

speaker: CG
words: I couldn't say anything that she wasn't on my neck about it! And it

speaker: CG
words: left me without a mind. If I'd say anything I'd be reprimanded for it.

speaker: CG
words: So, I got so that for years I just kept still. Years on end. And then

speaker: CG
words: I had to stay with my grandfather and grandmother for quite a long time.

speaker: CG
words: They were born back in the 1812 '24- so they weren't latish by any

speaker: CG
words: means. And Grandmother, as nearly as I could make out, was firmly con

speaker: CG
words: vinced that a child is a visitation of Providence on adults in which re

speaker: CG
words: taliation for their sins! That is what I always thought Grandmother

speaker: CG
words: thought of a child. Well, —(bell rings) thought that they were sent

speaker: CG
words: to punish for— And she had only twelve! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Maybe that's why she started thinking that.

speaker: CG
words: There were two of them that died when they were babies. And there was

speaker: CG
words: one that died when she was, oh, I think about fourteen— twelve or four

speaker: CG
words: teen, something like that— and then there was Uncle John, who with

speaker: CG
words: Uncle James went into the Civil War; and Uncle James got home, thanks

speaker: CG
words: to the ministration of some Catholic Sisters at one time. But, Uncle

speaker: CG
words: John was, for some reason or other, in a camp in Arkansas. I don't know

speaker: CG
words: whether it was a hospital camp or what it was, but, anyway, at one time

speaker: CG
words: the whole camp was down with measles. There were not well men enough

speaker: CG
words: for night guard with the number of measles they had. Well, Uncle John

speaker: CG
words: had measles, and the first day that he was up they put him out on night

speaker: CG
words: guard, because there was nobody else who was up out of his bed. And

speaker: CG
words: there was a cold rain falling. Well, he stood his guard for his appoint

speaker: CG
words: ed hours, and went back and went to bed and died. Well, Grandmother

speaker: CG
words: thought that the girl who died at ten, or twelve or whatever— I don't

speaker: CG
words: know just how old she was— and that Uncle John were pretty good. And

speaker: CG
words: then there was one girl and one boy that she was proud of. And I never

speaker: CG
words: could see why she was proud of the girl, excepting that the girl would

speaker: CG
words: stand up to her and lay down the law to her as to what she could say to

speaker: CG
words: this girl or couldn't. And it was that way. Well, I noticed afterwards,

speaker: CG
words: I didn't know this girl until she was married and had children of my

speaker: CG
words: age, but I noticed that this older sister of mine would stand up to

speaker: CG
words: Grandmother, and Grandmother admired her and liked her. She hated the

speaker: CG
words: very ground I walked on! She spoke nicely to me once during the time

speaker: CG
words: I was there, and I was positive that Grandfather had laid down the law

speaker: CG
words: to her. When he laid down the law, he was very gentle, very quiet about,t

speaker: CG
words: but, oh, how you did jump!

speaker: SS
words: Was this Grandmother the same Grandmother who was the great gardener

speaker: SS
words: you told me about before?

speaker: CG
words: She was law in her province, and he was law in his, which overstepped

speaker: CG
words: hers.

speaker: SS
words: Was she the one that was the gardener that you told me about? Was she

speaker: SS
words: the one that gardened, that had the green thumb?

speaker: CG
words: She was the gardener. Yes, she was the gardener.

speaker: CG
words: Nature had intended her for a gardner.

speaker: SS
words: You told me all about that in the first part.

speaker: SS
words: When you were a child the idea of bringing up children then and the way

speaker: SS
words: you were brought up, was it extremely different, do you think than the

speaker: SS
words: way it is now?

speaker: CG
words: I would say that the child was different, as well as the way it was

speaker: CG
words: brought up.i see a distinct difference between people now and long ago.

speaker: CG
words: And, long ago, a child spoke when he was spoken to; it wasn't that

speaker: CG
words: strict altogether, but it was with some families. But if a child went

speaker: CG
words: visiting, and the children did go visiting with the parents, babysitter

speaker: CG
words: hadn't been invented at that time, if you went visitinO, of course, you

speaker: CG
words: took the children. What else would you do with them? And the child

speaker: CG
words: would sit up on a chair, and easy chairs were not common,^with it's feet

speaker: CG
words: sticking straight out before it, and he sat there and kept still unless

speaker: CG
words: fr- was spoken to! Do you know a child now that would do that? Well,

speaker: CG
words: the children themselves were different, else they couldn't have done it.

speaker: CG
words: They couldn't have made them like that if they'd been just like the child

speaker: CG
words: ren now. In my opinion, this restlessness that we have now, was delib

speaker: CG
words: erately created by the subversive elements to stop people from thinking

speaker: CG
words: about what the subversive elements are doing. Keep them busy with non

speaker: CG
words: sense! Make them restless. And, a scientist on the radio said that

speaker: CG
words: the chemical effect of the chlorine that is put in the water, and which

speaker: CG
words: people are compelled to drink, whether^or no, the chemical effect of it

speaker: CG
words: on the person who drinks it, is to cause him to lose a sense of respon

speaker: CG
words: sibility, and that you can't boil it out of the water. I firmly believe

speaker: CG
words: that.

speaker: CG
words: I have this little incident about diverting people's minds. During

speaker: CG
words: the First World War, north of Spokane on a homestead was an uncle of

speaker: CG
words: mine and his wife, I had known all of her life, she was much younger

speaker: CG
words: than he was— which has nothing to do with the story—. But, they were

speaker: CG
words: living up there on the homestead and before the baby came the wife's

speaker: CG
words: mother went there to be with them, and just before the baby was born,

speaker: CG
words: this uncle had a stroke and he never really recovered from it, it dam

speaker: CG
words: aged his mind and body both, Well, here they were;as soon as the baby

speaker: CG
words: was born, this wife had to go to work to support them. And that left

speaker: CG
words: the old mother there to take care of this man who had had a stroke and

speaker: CG
words: was more or less helpless, and the new baby and the housework and all

speaker: CG
words: that. And it was during the First World War, and this Mrs. Lewer dec

speaker: CG
words: ided that she must do something to help the war effort, like the women

speaker: CG
words: were doing, they had the supply depots everywhere, and you got yar^

speaker: CG
words: there to knot socks and sweaters or you got handkerchiefs to hem or

speaker: CG
words: whatever they decided on at the time. One time they were making dresses

speaker: CG
words: for a certain country. And, this Mrs. Lewer went to the supply room

speaker: CG
words: because she thought she should do something in addition to all she was

speaker: CG
words: doing, to help the war effort. So, she went and asked for some handker

speaker: CG
words: chiefs to hem, which was what this supply depot was specialling at the

speaker: CG
words: timorv-so they gave her some and told her to hem them by hand and she

speaker: CG
words: said, "No, I'll hem them on the machine, because I can do so many more

speaker: CG
words: in the time to hem them on the machine." They said, "We will not accept

speaker: CG
words: them if they are hemmed on the machine. You are to hem them by hand."

speaker: CG
words: And she said, "Well, would you please tell me why I must hem them by

speaker: CG
words: hand?" They said, "Yes,"that they were doing that because it took more

speaker: CG
words: time and took up the women's attention from the boys who were gone.

speaker: CG
words: And it just took the women's attention to hem those handkerchiefs. A

speaker: CG
words: boy in the trench looking to see if his handkerchief was hemmed by hand

speaker: CG
words: or machine! So, she said, "Well, I'm taking care of my practically

speaker: CG
words: helpless son-in-law and the baby and I won't do anything if I can't

speaker: CG
words: hem them on the machine." She laid them down and walked out. And, I

speaker: CG
words: was in a supply depot in Columbus, Montana. I had taken in a sweater

speaker: CG
words: that a woman was knitting and didn't want to finish it, so I finished

speaker: CG
words: it for her and then I took it into the supply depot. Well, the woman

speaker: CG
words: accepted it from me and she picked up another sweater that was lying

speaker: CG
words: on the table that someone else had brought in, and she examined every

speaker: CG
words: part of it with the utmost care, and finally almost at the end of the

speaker: CG
words: knitting, right at the bottom of the sweater, almost finished, she

speaker: CG
words: found one stitch that turned a little bit crosswise from what it should

speaker: CG
words: have been. That was all that ailed the sweater for a boy in a trench.

speaker: CG
words: I wonder if he'd have found that stitch! But, anyway, she handed this

speaker: CG
words: sweater to the girl who had come in to help her. And, the girl looked

speaker: CG
words: over the sweater and finally she found , stitch and she said she would

speaker: CG
words: take the sweater home with her and she could fix that stitch, turn it

speaker: CG
words: smooth like it should be without pulling it out at all. And the woman

speaker: CG
words: said, "No, you pull that sweater out and wind the yarn." And the girl

speaker: CG
words: said, "Well, I could rip it back just that far." Just about an inch.

speaker: CG
words: She said, "Rip it back just that far and fix that stitch and then I'd

speaker: CG
words: knit it again." She said, "I told you to pull that yarn out and wind

speaker: CG
words: it and you pull it out and wind it!" Ah, ha, somebodjjelse would knit

speaker: CG
words: that same yarn. Perhaps it had been knitted before.

speaker: CG
words: It's a crime to work on people's minds that way, and it's a little

speaker: CG
words: witless for the people to eat it up like they do.

speaker: SS
words: A little what?

speaker: CG
words: Witless!

speaker: SS
words: Witless.

speaker: CG
words: Not having wits!

speaker: SS
words: I'm just curious. I want to ask you about World War I, a little more

speaker: SS
words: than this. First I do want to ask you this. I wonder, who do you feel

speaker: SS
words: the subversives are? Who are they? What are they trying to do?

speaker: CG
words: In my opinion it's the Communists. We are told over and over that we

speaker: CG
words: have a Communist United States Congress. That we have many Communists

speaker: CG
words: in there. In my mail today I had—of course, everything you get is a re

speaker: CG
words: quest for funds— this was a request for funds to work against the Com

speaker: CG
words: munists in the Congress.

speaker: SS
words: Let me ask you about World War I. These groups that— what did you call

speaker: SS
words: them? The stations where—

speaker: CG
words: Oh, the supply stations. The supply depots.

speaker: SS
words: Who organized them?

speaker: CG
words: I don't know who organized them. They were a Federal project. But who

speaker: CG
words: organized them, I never knew.

speaker: SS
words: But the women who were involved— was most of it done through the Red

speaker: SS
words: Cross? Or were there all kinds of groups?

speaker: CG
words: I think this was strictly Federal. The Red Cross did work. And one

speaker: CG
words: man who had been in the trenches said that all of these efforts that

speaker: CG
words: were done, only the Salvation Army came out clean. There was a great

speaker: CG
words: parade and celebration on the arrival of a ship at Frisco that bore the—

speaker: CG
words: what is this young men's organization?

speaker: SS
words: The YMCA?

speaker: CG
words: Yes. It was bringing home the YMCA, and they were honoring them, and

speaker: CG
words: this man who had been in the trenches, told me that the YMCA was there

speaker: CG
words: only to make money. He said that a boy would go to a counter, and well,

speaker: CG
words: he'd buy some candy or a drink or whatever was for sale there, and he'd

speaker: CG
words: lay down a dollar and he'd wait for his change and he didn't get it and

speaker: CG
words: he'd ask for it. "You don't get any change in here!" He said you

speaker: CG
words: couldn't lay down any bill or coin and get any change back on anything

speaker: CG
words: the YMCA. And, he said that they were just a hoot to the boys who

speaker: CG
words: knew them there at the trenches. But, he said that there was a little

speaker: CG
words: Salvation Army girl in her bonnet and her regalia that, with the

speaker: CG
words: mortars going, would come right down in the trench with her pie or what

speaker: CG
words: ever and she'd say, "Have a piece of pie with me!" "But I haven't any

speaker: CG
words: money." "You don't need money, take the pie." And that's the way the

speaker: CG
words: Salvation Army worked. And, he said, they were the only ones that were

speaker: CG
words: clean. And I knew a woman, she was neighbor to me in Moore,Montana,

speaker: CG
words: and she boasted about her brother was a worker in the supply depot in

speaker: CG
words: Paris, and that he came home with thirteen pairs of socks and eight

speaker: CG
words: sweaters from the supply depot. We have these things, and we tolerate

speaker: CG
words: them. And we have our chlorine to make us dumb.

speaker: SS
words: Are you very familiar with the Salvation Army in the early days? With

speaker: SS
words: the work that they did?

speaker: CG
words: I have known about the Salvation Army for, well, I don't know, it's a

speaker: CG
words: long time. And I had an apartment in DesMoines, Iowa, I had a room in

speaker: CG
words: the home of a Salvation Army couple, who took lodgers. And she took

speaker: CG
words: me to one of their meetings one Sunday while I was there. And she talked

speaker: CG
words: the Salvation Army quite a bit to me, and so I got a little more ac

speaker: CG
words: quainted with it. She tried to get me to join and I said, "No, I admire

speaker: CG
words: the Salvation Army and I always contribute to them when there's an op

speaker: CG
words: portunity because they will do the things that I wouldn't do, but I want

speaker: CG
words: them done!" Well, like going down the trenches with the mortars going!

speaker: CG
words: And they go everywhere, to everybody in any circumstance. Well, I think

speaker: CG
words: there are some places that maybe I'd refuse to go, where they go and

speaker: CG
words: under their bonnet* they're safe. They can go. But, I wouldn't join.

speaker: SS
words: When you say "under their bonnet they're safe," what do you mean by that?

speaker: CG
words: Oh, their insignia for the women is a certain bonnet that they wear.

speaker: CG
words: And they wear that all of the time when they're out. And, they used

speaker: CG
words: to wear, well, certain regalia for dress that was a little distinctive.

speaker: CG
words: I don't think they do now. And I don't believe they wear the bonnet

speaker: CG
words: now; I don't know. But anyway, a woman under a Salvation Army bonnet

speaker: CG
words: was safe anywhere. They just didn't dare! But I was much interested

speaker: CG
words: in this meeting. There was one young man that I noticed especially.

speaker: CG
words: He was quite young and he was somewhat deformed and diminutive, and the

speaker: CG
words: enjoyment that he was getting out of that meeting was just worth seeing.

speaker: CG
words: It was marvelous how he was enjoying that. Well, he was there where

speaker: CG
words: he was just as good as anybody else!! And, you know very well that isn't

speaker: CG
words: always true.^ But, he was just as good as anybody else and just like

speaker: CG
words: the rest of them. And, I enjoyed their meeting very much. It was most late

speaker: CG
words: ly singing and talking. There wasn't anything that you'd call a sermon

speaker: CG
words: or anything. But, it was folksy; it was pleasant.

speaker: SS
words: Were they centered in the cities, and not very much in the small country

speaker: SS
words: towns, or were they all over?

speaker: CG
words: They get everywhere. We entertained one woman, my husband and in our

speaker: CG
words: home for a day or two when she was at Moore, Montana, which was just a

speaker: CG
words: little kind of a whistle stop by the railroad, and she was working there.

speaker: CG
words: And she had her, oh, I don't know, banjo or something, whatever it was,

speaker: CG
words: and she'd sing for us, and she'd play, and she worked through the coun

speaker: CG
words: try there quite a while stopping with one place and another wherever

speaker: CG
words: they would take her in. In my opinion, the Salvation Army was and is

speaker: CG
words: the cleanest of all organizations. They seem to have no thought for

speaker: CG
words: anybody but the other fellow; they don't seem to think of themselves

speaker: CG
words: at all. And, their vows are very stfTct. And if one of them would marry

speaker: CG
words: outside of the Army, he's no more a Salvationist. He's put out. I

speaker: CG
words: I knew of a case of that in Colfax. At a place where I lodged, there

speaker: CG
words: was a young man who had been, and was then, in the Salvation Army, but

speaker: CG
words: the young man had become attracted to a girl who was not a Salvation

speaker: CG
words: ist. And I couldn't help hearing the conversation, they were talking

speaker: CG
words: in the hall right outside of my door, and the offical^Jiad come to talk

speaker: CG
words: with the young man, and he finally said, "Then you are determined that

speaker: CG
words: you will marry this woman?" And the boy said, "Yes," he would. So he

speaker: CG
words: was out.

speaker: SS
words: Is this Colfax, Washington?

speaker: CG
words: Colfax, Washington. But he was released from the Army.

speaker: SS
words: Was there an active chapter in Moscow, too?

speaker: CG
words: We had a Salvation officer, Captain; they have military titles. We

speaker: CG
words: had a Salvation Captain and his wife and they had one child and soon

speaker: CG
words: would have another, and I am positive that the city put them out of

speaker: CG
words: Moscow, just before this second one would be born. They had to move

speaker: CG
words: out of Moscow. And, I am positive that I am correct about it, that the

speaker: CG
words: city put them out.

speaker: SS
words: Because?

speaker: CG
words: Because it was Salvation Army!

speaker: SS
words: They didn't like the Salvation Army here?

speaker: CG
words: There was no other reason. They were just quiet, respectable citizens.

speaker: SS
words: Where I grew up, the Salvation Army had a good reputation. Very highly

speaker: SS
words: thought of, so I'm surprised that in Msocow they didn't feel the same

speaker: SS
words: way.

speaker: CG
words: Well, maybe Moscow's different than it used to be. I find it so. I

speaker: CG
words: find that Moscow is a very different place than it used to be, and a

speaker: CG
words: very pleasant place to live right now. One woman wanted to know one

speaker: CG
words: day why I didn't go somewhere. Why I lived here. "Well," I said, "when I find a better

speaker: CG
words: place, I might go."

speaker: SS
words: What you're saying is that in the early days that the Salvation Army

speaker: SS
words: wasn't always always liked, because they didn't want them here. I'm

speaker: SS
words: surprised— I mean—nothing surprises me about the early days, because

speaker: SS
words: things were very different then. But, I would think that they were such

speaker: SS
words: a good Christian organization that there would be no reason to be op

speaker: SS
words: posed to them.

speaker: CG
words: I never knew— that is to say— I never legally knew— legal, either

speaker: CG
words: you saw it, or experienced it or something— well, I never legally knew

speaker: CG
words: about this, but I knew that they had to leave, because I talked with

speaker: CG
words: the Captain himself. And the way he wouldn't say anothing was quite a

speaker: CG
words: work of art!. AHe wouldn't say anything!

speaker: SS
words: They had a Captain, did that mean they had a whole group here, or was it

speaker: SS
words: just the one man?

speaker: CG
words: It was just the one.

speaker: SS
words: What would he do all by himself, without having a ?

speaker: CG
words: I don't know just what his activities were. I just don't know. I

speaker: CG
words: was more or less a stranger here myself at the time. I didn't get

speaker: CG
words: into things much, and I don't know just what he did. I don't believe

speaker: CG
words: they had a kettle here last Christmas, I don't think they did. But

speaker: CG
words: there was a Christmas recently, I think it was year before last or the

speaker: CG
words: one before that.

speaker: SS
words: I don't know, they might have had one last year. It seems to me like

speaker: SS
words: they did.

speaker: CG
words: Well, maybe they did.

speaker: SS
words: I can't be sure now, because one year and another one— it's hard to

speaker: SS
words: remember.

speaker: CG
words: I know they have had their kettle here with its bell.

speaker: SS
words: That's what Iremember from when Iwas a little kid, is the kettle at

speaker: SS
words: Christmastime. You just mentioned him getting sort of pushed out of

speaker: SS
words: town. When you were talking to me the last time about the opposition

speaker: SS
words: of businessmen to you in Troy in the newspaper, I thought about that

speaker: SS
words: after. You didn't say how you knew that they didn't want you in town.

speaker: SS
words: What did they do so that you knew that you weren't—?

speaker: CG
words: Is this going on the tape?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, is that wrong?

speaker: CG
words: Well, I don't want to discuss that on the tape.

speaker: SS
words: Okay.

speaker: CG
words: Always. There was no such thing as paying somebody to take care of you

speaker: CG
words: when you were sick. I know my grandmother was telling about—she told

speaker: CG
words: this to her granddaughters as a pattern of what women should be— and

speaker: CG
words: she said that woman would get her own breakfast and the morning work

speaker: CG
words: all done up, and she would take her baby on her hip and walk a mile and

speaker: CG
words: a half across the fields to where there was a sick woman, and she would

speaker: CG
words: take care of her and do up her work and then she'd go home again in time

speaker: CG
words: to get dinner for her man. Did that day after day as a matter of course!

speaker: CG
words: That ^the waY people did. My grandfather was telling about— one time

speaker: CG
words: there was a young woman who was sick abed, and there was nobody to stay

speaker: CG
words: with her and two young men went and sat with her through the night. Well,

speaker: CG
words: that was somebody with her! And Grandfather told also about a fellow

speaker: CG
words: who was sick and two of the others had gone to take care of him, and

speaker: CG
words: they were giving him quinine, and he said it was just so bad he couldn't

speaker: CG
words: take any more of it. And so one of them said, "Well, I'll fix it so

speaker: CG
words: that you can take it and it won't bother you."

speaker: CG
words: So, he went to the kit

speaker: CG
words: chen and he got an apple and scraped two tablespoonsful of scraped apple

speaker: CG
words: and he put the quinine covered up in one of them and then he took them

speaker: CG
words: back to the man in bed and he said, "Now, I've got a spoonful of apple

speaker: CG
words: here for you to take after you take the quinine, and then you won't tas

speaker: CG
words: te it, because it'll take the taste right out of your mouth." So, he

speaker: CG
words: says, "I'll give you the apple." And he gave him the quinine— he gave

speaker: CG
words: him the apple first, and then he gave him the quinine and he swalLed

speaker: CG
words: the quinine right down and he said, "That did work!" (Chuckles) And,

speaker: CG
words: there was a case at— In the Garneill country, not more than seventeen

speaker: CG
words: or eighteen miles from Garneill, which was the post office— there was

speaker: CG
words: a family that had lived there and had moved away and one young woman in

speaker: CG
words: the family died after they had been gone a while, and they wanted her

speaker: CG
words: to go back to the burial ground by the Clifford School, because that

speaker: CG
words: was their plot when they had lived there. So, they sent the body back

speaker: CG
words: but there was nobody who could accompany it, so the people there received

speaker: CG
words: it and put it in the schoolhouse over night, and they would have the

speaker: CG
words: service the next day And at that time if there was^death, al

speaker: CG
words: ways someone sat with the corpse through the night. Well, they searched

speaker: CG
words: the neighborhood, there weren't many people there at that time, it was

speaker: CG
words: long ago in the very early days, and there was not a woman anywhere who

speaker: CG
words: could go. There was one who was unable herself to go, and there was

speaker: CG
words: another one whose husband was away and she had young children at home.

speaker: CG
